topics.title: Overview of Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or **motes**) that use sensors to cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations. Each node in a WSN is equipped with a **radio transceiver**, a **microcontroller**, an **energy source** (usually a battery), and one or more **sensing elements**, and they collaborate to forward sensed data through the network to a **sink node** (also called the **base station** or **gateway**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"A Wireless Sensor Network is a collection of a large number of sensor nodes that are densely deployed either inside the phenomenon or very close to it, forming a self-organizing network through wireless communication."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forest Fire Watcher" Intuition

Imagine a forest where the government wants to detect fires as early as possible. Instead of installing expensive satellite cameras, they scatter **thousands of tiny, cheap, battery-powered heat detectors** throughout the forest. Each detector:

1. Senses temperature continuously.
2. Talks to its nearest neighbor (no central tower needed).
3. Passes an alert message along a chain ("fire here!") until it reaches the forest ranger's command center.

If a tree falls and blocks one detector, the network **automatically reroutes** the message through other detectors — there is no fixed path, no central controller, and no manual repair. This is exactly what a WSN does in an industrial, military, or smart-city deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Beginners:** Think of a WSN as a *team of tiny, mute, energy-frugal helpers* that whisper to each other about what they sense, collectively delivering one big report to a central receiver.

### 1.3 Standard WSN Metrics & Terminology

The following standardized metrics are used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus for WSN evaluation:

- **Mote / Sensor Node**: A single autonomous sensing device.
- **Sink / Base Station (BS)**: The data collection point that aggregates all sensor reports.
- **Sensor Field**: The physical area where sensor nodes are deployed.
- **Event Detection**: The phenomenon being monitored (e.g., temperature > 50°C).
- **Multi-hop Communication**: Data travels through several intermediate nodes before reaching the sink.
- **Energy per Bit (Epb)**: Measured in **Joules/bit (J/bit)** or **nanojoules/bit (nJ/bit)**.
- **Network Lifetime**: The operational duration until the first/last node dies; typically measured in **days**, **months**, or **years**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Line KTU Favourite:** *"WSNs shift the paradigm from networking a few powerful computers to coordinating thousands of constrained, energy-limited devices."*

### 1.4 Comparison: WSN vs. Classical Wired/Ad-hoc Networks

| Feature | Wired Network | Mobile Ad-hoc Network (MANET) | Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node Count | Tens to hundreds | Tens to hundreds | **Hundreds to thousands** |
| Node Mobility | Static | Highly mobile | Mostly **static / limited mobility** |
| Energy | Powered (mains) | Rechargeable battery | **Strict energy constraint (non-rechargeable often)** |
| Data Flow | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Mostly **many-to-one (sensor → sink)** |
| Node Identity | Unique IP/MAC | Unique IP/MAC | Often **address-centric protocols are inefficient**; data-centric |
| Deployment | Planned | Planned/Semi-random | Often **random / drop-and-forget** |
| Application | Generic | Generic | **Application-specific** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks: *"How is a WSN different from a MANET?"* — The strongest differentiator is **energy scarcity + data-centric (not address-centric) routing + scale (density)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Sensor Node's Hardware

Every sensor node — irrespective of vendor (MicaZ, TelosB, IRIS, etc.) — has **four mandatory functional units**:

1. **Sensing Unit**
   - Comprises one or more **sensors** (e.g., LM35 for temperature, MQ-2 for gas) and an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
   - Converts physical phenomena into digital signals.

2. **Processing Unit**
   - A **microcontroller** (e.g., MSP430, ATmega128) with limited RAM and ROM.
   - Runs the operating system (typically **TinyOS**) and executes MAC/routing protocols.

3. **Transceiver Unit**
   - A short-range **radio** (e.g., CC2420 operating at **2.4 GHz**, IEEE 802.15.4 compliant).
   - Responsible for wireless communication.

4. **Power Unit**
   - A battery (often **AA alkaline** or **lithium coin cell**).
   - *Optional*: Energy harvesting sub-unit (solar, vibration, RF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Distribution Reality Check (KTU High-Yield):** In a typical sensor node, **communication (transceiver) consumes more than 70%** of total energy, while sensing and processing consume less than 30%. This is why KTU protocol design questions always emphasize *minimizing radio-on time*.

### 2.2 Operational Steps in a WSN

The functional flow of a WSN can be broken down into the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Deployment:** Nodes are scattered (randomly, deterministically, or by mobility-assisted means) over the sensor field.
- **Step 2 — Self-Organization:** Nodes discover neighbors via beaconing and form an **ad-hoc topology**.
- **Step 3 — Sensing:** Each node periodically samples the environment based on its duty cycle.
- **Step 4 — Local Processing (In-network Aggregation):** Instead of forwarding raw data, nodes may compute aggregates (e.g., *average*, *max*, *min*) to reduce transmission volume.
- **Step 5 — Multi-hop Forwarding:** Packets hop toward the sink via intermediate relays.
- **Step 6 — Sink-side Analysis:** The BS performs event detection, fusion, and decision-making.

### 2.3 WSN Communication Stack (Cross-Layer View)

Unlike the strict OSI model, WSN protocols are often **cross-layered** for efficiency:

| Layer (Traditional) | WSN Equivalent | Example Protocols (KTU High-Yield) |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Task-specific code | Surveillance, tracking apps |
| Transport | Lightweight reliability | **DCCP-like**, PSFQ, RMST |
| Network | Data-centric routing | **SPIN**, **Directed Diffusion**, **LEACH**, **PEGASIS** |
| Data Link (MAC) | Energy-efficient MAC | **S-MAC**, **B-MAC**, **IEEE 802.15.4** |
| Physical | Low-power radio | **CC1000**, **CC2420** (2.4 GHz O-QPSK) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cross-Layer?** A sensor node cannot afford the overhead of seven independent protocol stacks. Energy-aware routing, MAC duty cycling, and application semantics are jointly optimized.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are core to Module 1 problems in KTU examinations.

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| First Order Radio Energy (transmit) | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | $k$ = bits; $d$ = distance (m); $n \in [2,4]$ |
| First Order Radio Energy (receive) | $E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit |
| Free-space path loss exponent | $n = 2$ | Short distance, no obstacles |
| Multi-path path loss exponent | $n = 4$ | Long distance, urban indoor |
| Energy to run electronics | $E_{elec}$ = **50 nJ/bit** | Typical Mica mote value |
| Transmit amplifier (free space) | $\varepsilon_{fs}$ = **10 pJ/bit/m²** | For $n = 2$ |
| Transmit amplifier (multi-path) | $\varepsilon_{mp}$ = **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** | For $n = 4$ |
| Network Lifetime | $L = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{consumed\ per\ round}}$ | Number of operational rounds |
| Optimal Cluster Head fraction (LEACH) | $p_{opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | $N$ = nodes; $M$ = field side |
| Throughput per node | $S = \dfrac{(1 - p)(1 - a)}{(1 - p) + Npa(1 + a)}$ | $p$ = collision prob; $a$ = vulnerable period ratio |
| Energy Harvesting Rate | $P_{harvest} = \eta \cdot P_{incident}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (0 to 1) |
| Sleep-to-Active Ratio | $D_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Typical: 1–10% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Constraints:**
> - The transmit energy equation assumes **no retransmission** — actual consumed energy is higher if ARQ is used.
> - $d^{2}$ model holds when $d < d_{cross\ over}$; beyond that, use $d^{4}$.
> - **Cluster-head election is randomized** every round (LEACH) to balance energy load uniformly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

WSNs are deployed today in:
- **Precision Agriculture** — soil moisture monitoring, smart irrigation.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** — bridges, dams, skyscrapers (vibration/tilting sensors).
- **Healthcare** — body area networks (BANs) for patient monitoring.
- **Military / Border Surveillance** — intrusion detection, battlefield awareness.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — predictive maintenance, smart factories.
- **Environmental Monitoring** — wildlife tracking, forest fire, volcanic eruption prediction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Module 1 lays the foundation for Module 2 (MAC protocols) and Module 3 (Routing). A clear grasp of *why energy is the bottleneck* is the prerequisite for every protocol critique in the syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman, 2000)

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 syllabus. It appears almost every semester in Part B questions.

**Statement:** *For transmitting a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$, the total energy dissipated is the sum of the energy to run the transmitter electronics and the energy to amplify the signal.*

**Step 1 — Decompose total transmit energy**

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k,d)
$$

Here, the first term is the *base energy* to run the digital electronics (encoding, modulation, filtering), and the second term is the *amplifier energy* required to ensure adequate signal strength at distance $d$.

**Step 2 — Express each term**

$$
E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

$$
E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k,d) = \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

The amplifier energy scales with distance raised to the **path-loss exponent $n$**. For free-space propagation, $n = 2$; for obstructed/multi-path, $n = 4$.

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Receiver energy (no amplifier, only electronics)**

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is asymmetric:** Receiving consumes energy too! Many students forget this in exams. A node listening to a 1-kbit packet spends ~50 nJ even if it ignores the data. This is why overhearing avoidance is critical in WSN MAC design.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Energy Per Round

**Problem:** A sensor node transmits a 2000-bit packet to a CH at 30 m, and the CH transmits it to the BS at 90 m. Compute total energy per round using the free-space model.

**Given:**
- $k = 2000$ bits
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free space)
- $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multi-path)

**Step 1 — Determine $d_{crossover}$**

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{0.0013}} \approx \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Node → CH transmission (30 m, free space)**

$$
E_{Tx1} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx1} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(30)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + (20 \times 10^{-9})(900)
$$

$$
E_{Tx1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 1.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — CH receives the packet**

$$
E_{Rx1} = E_{elec} \cdot k = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — CH → BS transmission (90 m, multi-path)**

Since $90 \text{ m} > d_{crossover} = 87.7 \text{ m}$, we use the multi-path model:

$$
E_{Tx2} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}
$$

$$
E_{Tx2} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(90)^{4}
$$

$$
E_{Tx2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + (2.6 \times 10^{-15})(6.561 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
E_{Tx2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.706 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.0017 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Total energy consumed in this round**

$$
E_{total} = E_{Tx1} + E_{Rx1} + E_{Tx2}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 1.18 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0017 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 3.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J per round}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Notice how the $d^{4}$ term amplifies even a slightly longer hop enormously. This is the foundation of the LEACH clustering idea — *avoid long hops* by introducing aggregators.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — WSN Design Trade-offs

| Design Goal | Solution | Trade-off / Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| Maximize lifetime | Duty-cycle nodes (sleep mode) | Increased latency for event reporting |
| Improve reliability | Multi-path routing | Redundant transmissions = more energy |
| Reduce congestion | In-network aggregation | Loss of raw data granularity |
| Localization accuracy | More anchor nodes | Higher cost and deployment complexity |
| Coverage uniformity | Random uniform distribution | Cluster hot-spots by random chance |
| Scalability | Hierarchical clustering (LEACH) | Cluster head is a single point of failure |
| Security | Lightweight encryption (TinySec) | Extra CPU and energy per packet |

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Optimal Number of Clusters

**Derivation:** Minimize total energy by choosing optimal cluster count $k_{opt}$.

$$
E_{total} = k \cdot E_{CH} + N \cdot E_{non\text{-}CH}
$$

Setting the derivative $dE_{total}/dk = 0$ and solving gives:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

Where $N$ is the total number of nodes, $M$ is the field side length, and $d_{toBS}$ is the average distance to base station.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU numerical problems:** If $N = 100$, $M = 100$ m, $d_{toBS} = 75$ m, then $k_{opt} \approx 3$ to 5 clusters for a typical LEACH deployment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of a Single Sensor Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor 1 Temperature] --> ADC[ADC Module]
    B[Sensor 2 Humidity] --> ADC
    C[Sensor 3 Light] --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller MSP430]
    MCU --> MEM[Memory RAM/ROM]
    MCU --> TX[Transceiver CC2420 2.4 GHz]
    TX --> ANT[Antenna]
    MCU --> PWR[Power Unit Battery 2xAA]
    PWR -.->|DC-DC| MCU
    PWR -.->|DC-DC| TX
```

**Reading the diagram:** Sensor analog outputs (left) are digitized by the ADC, processed by the MCU (which uses memory for buffering), and transmitted by the radio through the antenna. The battery powers the MCU and transceiver via onboard DC-DC converters.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — WSN Communication Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIELD[Sensor Field Deployed Nodes] --> SENS[Sensing Layer]
    SENS --> PROC[In-Network Processing Aggregation]
    PROC --> ROUTE[Routing Layer Multi Hop]
    ROUTE --> SINK[Sink Node Base Station]
    SINK --> BACK[Backhaul Internet Satellite]
    BACK --> USER[End User Cloud Dashboard]

    PROC -.->|feedback| CLOCK[Duty Cycle Scheduler]
    CLOCK -.->|control| SENS
    ROUTE -.->|topology info| CLOCK
```

**Reading the diagram:** Nodes in the field sense → process locally → forward via multi-hop routing → sink collects → backhaul transmits to end user. The clock block is a feedback controller that schedules sleep/wake based on routing state.

### 4.3 Network Topology Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAR[Star Topology]
        S1[Node 1] --> BS[Base Station]
        S2[Node 2] --> BS
        S3[Node 3] --> BS
    end

    subgraph MESH[Mesh Topology]
        N1[Node 1] --- N2[Node 2]
        N2 --- N3[Node 3]
        N3 --- N4[Node 4]
        N4 --- N1
        N1 --- N3
    end

    subgraph TREE[Cluster Tree Hierarchical]
        CH1[Cluster Head 1] --> BS2[Base Station]
        M1[Member 1] --> CH1
        M2[Member 2] --> CH1
        CH2[Cluster Head 2] --> BS2
        M3[Member 3] --> CH2
        M4[Member 4] --> CH2
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** WSNs can adopt three primary topologies:
- **Star:** All nodes directly talk to BS — simple, but long-range transmissions drain energy.
- **Mesh:** Multi-hop, robust, but routing overhead is high.
- **Cluster Tree (Hierarchical):** Members talk to CHs, CHs forward to BS — energy-efficient and scalable (LEACH, PEGASIS).

### 4.4 WSN Protocol Stack vs. OSI Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application Layer] --> TRN[Transport Layer]
    TRN --> NET[Network Layer]
    NET --> MAC[MAC Sublayer]
    MAC --> PHY[Physical Radio]
    APP -.- APP_R[Task Scheduling]
    NET -.- NET_R[Energy Aware Routing]
    MAC -.- MAC_R[Sleep Wakeup]
    PHY -.- PHY_R[Transmit Power Control]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four dotted cross-layer interactions are unique to WSNs. Classical OSI does not allow layer skipping; WSNs explicitly couple, e.g., *application duty cycle* with *MAC sleep cycles* to save energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Hint:** When asked *"Why is cross-layer design used in WSNs?"* — answer: *"To reduce energy consumption and protocol overhead by sharing state across traditional layer boundaries."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List four key characteristics that distinguish a WSN from a traditional wired network.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-organizing, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that collaboratively sense, process, and forward data over a wireless medium to a sink node.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**
1. **Energy constrained** — nodes are battery-powered; energy is the primary design metric.
2. **Data-centric** — communication is based on data attributes (e.g., "temperature > 50°C"), not node addresses.
3. **Application-specific** — hardware, software, and protocols are tailored to the deployment scenario.
4. **Dense deployment with high redundancy** — hundreds to thousands of nodes, often randomly placed, tolerate individual failures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain the four major components of a sensor node with a neat block diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Sensing Unit** — sensors + ADC; converts physical phenomena to digital signals.
2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + memory; executes protocols and locally aggregates data.
3. **Transceiver Unit** — radio (e.g., CC2420); handles wireless transmit/receive.
4. **Power Unit** — battery ± energy harvester; the single most critical resource.

*(A small ASCII or textual block diagram earns the 3rd mark — describe the four blocks connected as sensing → processing → transceiver, with power tapped to processing and transceiver.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a sensor node. Discuss the role of each subsystem and explain why energy is the most critical design constraint.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the first-order radio energy model for a WSN. For a node transmitting 4000 bits over 50 m using the free-space model, compute the transmit energy assuming $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m².

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram above. Describe:
- **Sensing unit** senses physical phenomena and converts them via ADC.
- **Processing unit** runs TinyOS, performs aggregation, compression, encryption.
- **Transceiver unit** uses IEEE 802.15.4 radio at 2.4 GHz, supports O-QPSK modulation.
- **Power unit** supplies 2–3 V from AA batteries, often with a DC-DC regulator.

**Why energy is critical:**
- Nodes are deployed in hostile/inaccessible areas — battery replacement is impossible.
- Communication dominates energy budget (often > 70%).
- Network lifetime is bounded by the first node's energy exhaustion.
- Protocols (MAC, routing, application) must be energy-aware.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Derivation as in Section 3.1:

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

Substitute $k = 4000$, $d = 50$, $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$:

$$
E_{Tx} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(50)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + (40 \times 10^{-9})(2500)
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Answer: $E_{Tx} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J = 0.3 mJ.**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Wireless Sensor Networks with Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANETs) across at least five parameters. Explain why traditional MANET routing protocols (e.g., AODV, DSR) are inefficient for WSNs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN of 100 nodes is uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Compute the optimal number of clusters using the LEACH criterion. Assume $d_{toBS} = 75$ m, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | WSN | MANET |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | 100s–1000s | 10s–100s |
| Energy | Critical, often non-rechargeable | Less critical, rechargeable |
| Mobility | Mostly static | Highly mobile |
| Identity | Data-centric | Address-centric (IP-like) |
| Traffic pattern | Many-to-one (convergecast) | Peer-to-peer |
| Application | Sensing-specific | Generic data communication |

**Why AODV/DSR fail in WSNs:**
- They use **address-based routing**, causing excessive route-setup overhead in dense WSNs.
- They are designed for **mobility**, not energy.
- They maintain **end-to-end routes**, which require constant beaconing (energy expensive).
- WSN needs **data-centric**, **energy-aware**, **in-network aggregation** — none of which AODV provides.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using the optimal cluster formula:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

Substitute $N = 100$, $M = 100$, $d_{toBS} = 75$:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \cdot \dfrac{100}{75^{2}}
$$

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{15.92} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot \dfrac{100}{5625}
$$

$$
k_{opt} \approx 3.99 \cdot 87.71 \cdot 0.01778 \approx 6.22
$$

**Answer: Approximately 5–6 optimal clusters.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in WSN Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the receive energy.** Many students write $E_{Tx}$ only and forget $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$. This costs **1–2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using the wrong path-loss exponent.** If $d > d_{crossover}$, you MUST use $n = 4$, not $n = 2$. Examiners specifically test this transition.
> 3. **Conflating WSN with MANET.** Saying *"WSNs and MANETs are the same"* will cost full marks on comparison questions. Always emphasize *energy*, *scale*, and *data-centric* differences.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers.** A correct number without J/m/nJ/m² units attracts *partial* credit only.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw block diagrams** in 7-mark architecture questions — even a hand-drawn ASCII box earns the 3rd mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** WSN = self-organizing, energy-constrained, data-centric network of spatially distributed motes.
- [ ] **Four blocks of a node:** Sensing + Processing + Transceiver + Power.
- [ ] **Energy equation:** $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$, where $n \in \{2,4\}$.
- [ ] **Crossover distance:** $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m (standard values).
- [ ] **Standard values:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit; $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²; $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.
- [ ] **Communication is the dominant energy consumer** (> 70% of node energy).
- [ ] **Cross-layer design** is mandatory in WSNs to minimize overhead.
- [ ] **Data-centric vs. address-centric** routing is the most cited WSN vs. MANET difference.
- [ ] **Hierarchical (cluster) topologies** save energy compared to flat or star topologies.
- [ ] **Network lifetime** is bounded by the energy exhaustion of the first node.
- [ ] **Applications:** Agriculture, healthcare, military, SHM, IIoT, environmental monitoring.
- [ ] **Radio standards:** IEEE 802.15.4 at 2.4 GHz with O-QPSK modulation; data rates up to 250 kbps.
- [ ] **Operating systems:** TinyOS is the canonical WSN OS (event-driven, component-based).
- [ ] **Duty cycling** is the primary lever for energy savings in idle/listening states.
- [ ] **In-network aggregation** reduces traffic at the cost of granularity.
- [ ] **Topologies:** Star, Mesh, Cluster-Tree (hierarchical).
- [ ] **Routing protocols to know:** SPIN, Directed Diffusion, LEACH, PEGASIS.
- [ ] **MAC protocols to know:** S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4.
- [ ] **Formula to remember:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d_{toBS}^{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Overview of Wireless Sensor Networks.**
> *For Module 2 (MAC Protocols) and Module 3 (Routing), refer to the next set of KTU 2024 notes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Node Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single-Node Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sensor Node** (also called a *mote*) is the fundamental building block of a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). It is a miniature, resource-constrained embedded device capable of sensing physical phenomena, performing local computation, and wirelessly communicating with neighboring nodes or a central sink.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMRT634, Module 1), the **Single-Node Architecture** refers to the internal organization of this individual node, which is conventionally decomposed into **four functional subunits**:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises the physical sensor(s) and an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC).
2. **Processing Unit** — Built around a microcontroller (or System-on-Chip) coupled with on-chip memory (RAM/ROM/Flash).
3. **Transceiver Unit** — A short-range radio (RF) responsible for wireless communication.
4. **Power Supply Unit** — Typically a battery (e.g., AA, coin cell, or energy-harvesting module).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A sensor node is *not* defined by what it senses — it is defined by its ability to operate **autonomously, unattended, and energy-neutrally** for extended periods (months to years) in harsh environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Watchman"

Imagine a **security guard** posted inside a vast factory:

| Watchman Action | Sensor Node Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Eyes & Ears watching the surroundings | **Sensors** (temperature, pressure, vibration) |
| Brain — remembers and decides whether to call for help | **Microcontroller** (processing + memory) |
| Walkie-Talkie to communicate with the central office | **RF Transceiver** |
| Lunch box supplying the day's energy | **Battery / Energy Harvester** |

The watchman does *not* need super-human strength — just reliability, vigilance, and energy efficiency. Similarly, a sensor node sacrifices raw computational power for **longevity, low cost, and tiny form factor**.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

Every KTU exam answer on Single-Node Architecture must remember these standard figures-of-merit:

- **Operating Voltage:** typically **2.0 V – 3.6 V** (for modern motes like MicaZ, TelosB).
- **Radio Frequency Bands:** **433 MHz / 868 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz** (ISM bands).
- **Data Rates:** **10 kbps – 250 kbps** (orders of magnitude lower than Wi-Fi).
- **Node Lifetime Target:** **≥ 2 years** on 2× AA alkaline cells (~3000 mAh).
- **Power Budget (Active Mode):** **~30 – 75 mW**.
- **Power Budget (Sleep Mode):** **~0.03 mW** (i.e., **30 µW**).

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio between *active* and *sleep* power (~1000×) is the **single most important design lever** for node lifetime — this is why duty cycling is a recurring 14-mark question in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Subunit Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Sensing Unit
- **Sensor (Analog Front-End):** Converts physical stimulus (temperature, humidity, light, acceleration) into a continuous analog voltage.
- **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Discretizes the analog signal into digital samples. Resolution is typically **8-bit, 10-bit, or 12-bit**.
- **Why it matters:** Sensor accuracy is bounded by both the physical transducer's sensitivity *and* the ADC's quantization noise:

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}$$

where $Q$ is the quantization step, $V_{\text{ref}}$ is the reference voltage, and $n$ is the ADC resolution in bits.

### 2.1.2 Processing Unit
- **Microcontroller (µC):** Executes the application logic, schedules radio activity, and runs the operating system (e.g., TinyOS, Contiki).
- **Memory Architecture:**
  - *Program Flash:* 32 KB – 1 MB (stores OS + application code).
  - *RAM:* 2 KB – 64 KB (runtime data, buffers).
  - *EEPROM/FRAM:* persistence for configuration.
- **Why µC, not µP?** A microcontroller integrates CPU + RAM + peripherals on a single die, drastically reducing static power.

### 2.1.3 Transceiver Unit
- **Operating States:** Transmit ($TX$), Receive ($RX$), Idle (listening, no decoding), and Sleep.
- **Key RF Parameters:** Carrier frequency, modulation (OOK, FSK, BPSK), transmit power $P_{TX}$, receiver sensitivity (typically **−85 dBm to −100 dBm**).
- **Why RF and not optical/acoustic?** RF is omni-directional and penetrates non-metallic obstacles — ideal for random deployments.

### 2.1.4 Power Supply Unit
- **Primary Cells:** Alkaline, Lithium Thionyl Chloride (Li-SOCl₂) — high energy density (~2400 Wh/kg for Li-SOCl₂).
- **Energy Harvesting:** Solar (PV), vibration (piezoelectric), thermal (TEG), RF — to extend lifetime indefinitely.

## 2.2 Operating System Requirements for WSNs

A WSN operating system is **not** a general-purpose OS. Its hard requirements are:

1. **Small Memory Footprint** — fits in < 64 KB ROM.
2. **Event-Driven Execution** — node sleeps until a hardware interrupt fires (timer, ADC ready, packet arrived).
3. **Energy Awareness** — exposes APIs to put peripherals in sleep states.
4. **Concurrency Support** — handles multiple sensor streams + radio I/O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examples (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **TinyOS** (UC Berkeley): Component-based, nesC language.
> - **Contiki** (SICS, Sweden): Protothreads, full IPv6 support (6LoWPAN).
> - **RIOT, Zephyr** — modern successors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{sense}} + E_{\text{process}} + E_{\text{comm}}$ | Total energy per packet | Joules (J) |
| $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$ | Transmit energy over distance $d$, $\alpha$=path-loss exponent | J |
| $E_{RX}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k$ | Receive energy (no amplification) | J |
| $\text{DC} = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | Duty Cycle (0 ≤ DC ≤ 1) | dimensionless |
| $P_{\text{avg}} = \text{DC} \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-\text{DC}) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$ | Average Power Consumption | W (Watts) |
| $L_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_{\text{day}}}$ | Node Lifetime (in days) | days |
| $\dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{P_{TX}}{P_{RX} \cdot L_{\text{path}}}$ | Link Budget (Friis model) | dimensionless |
| $Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}$ | ADC quantization step | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common $\alpha$ values (Path-Loss Exponent):**
> - Free space: $\alpha = 2$
> - Urban cellular: $\alpha \approx 3$ to $4$
> - Obstructed indoor: $\alpha \approx 4$ to $6$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-Node Architecture is not merely academic — it underpins:

- **Precision Agriculture** — MicaZ motes monitor soil moisture in vineyards (e.g., HP Labs, 2004 deployment).
- **Structural Health Monitoring** — vibration sensors on the Golden Gate Bridge.
- **Healthcare Body Area Networks (BAN)** — ECG patches on patients.
- **Military Surveillance** — acoustic gunshot localization in battlefields.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — predictive maintenance on rotating machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every one of these deployments, the **node lifetime** is the make-or-break metric. A node that lasts 2 weeks is rejected; one that lasts 2 years is deployed at scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section performs **exhaustive derivations** for two critical KTU problems: (i) average power consumption under duty cycling, and (ii) node lifetime estimation.

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Power Consumption under Duty Cycling

### Problem Setup
A sensor node has two power states:
- **Active state**: Power $P_{\text{active}}$, duration $T_{\text{active}}$
- **Sleep state**: Power $P_{\text{sleep}}$, duration $T_{\text{sleep}}$

We wish to find the **time-averaged power** drawn from the battery.

### Step 1 — Define the Period of Operation
A WSN node operates in a repeating cycle:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Energy per Cycle
By definition, energy = power × time:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}$$

### Step 3 — Divide Energy by Cycle Time
Average power is energy per unit time:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Duty Cycle Variable
By definition:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$$

This implies:

$$1 - \text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the Power Equation
Factor out $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} &= P_{\text{active}} \cdot \left(\frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}\right) + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot \left(\frac{T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= P_{\text{active}} \cdot \text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \\[6pt]
&= \text{DC} \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Sanity Check (Boundary Cases)

- **DC = 1** (node always active): $P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{active}}$ ✓
- **DC = 0** (node always sleeping): $P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{sleep}}$ ✓
- **DC = 0.01, $P_{\text{sleep}} \approx 0$**: $P_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.01 \cdot P_{\text{active}}$ — a **100× reduction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Halving the duty cycle does *not* halve the lifetime — it roughly **doubles** it, because the sleep power is negligible compared to active power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Transmit Energy over Distance $d$

### Problem Setup
A node transmits a $k$-bit packet to a neighbor at distance $d$. The energy model (Heinzelman-Leach model) splits energy into:

- **Electronics energy** $E_{\text{elec}}$ — dissipated in the digital circuitry (per bit, identical for TX and RX).
- **Amplifier energy** $E_{\text{amp}}$ — dissipated in the power amplifier (proportional to $d^{\alpha}$).

### Step 1 — Transmit Energy Formula (Standard Model)

$$E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$$

### Step 2 — Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Practice)

**Given:**
- $k = 2000$ bits
- $d = 10$ m
- $\alpha = 2$ (free space)
- $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $E_{\text{amp}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² (i.e., $100 \times 10^{-12}$)

**Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TX} &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (100 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(10^{2}) \\[6pt]
&= 100 \times 10^{-6} + (100 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(100) \\[6pt]
&= 100 \times 10^{-6} + 20000 \times 10^{-6} \\[6pt]
&= 100 \text{ µJ} + 20000 \text{ µJ} \\[6pt]
&= 20100 \text{ µJ} = 20.1 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- Digital electronics consume **100 µJ** (0.5 %).
- Amplifier consumes **20,000 µJ** (99.5 %).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** In free-space ($\alpha = 2$), the **power amplifier dominates**. This is why WSN MAC protocols *minimize transmit distance* — short hops are exponentially cheaper than long hops.

---

## 3.3 Derivations Summary Table (For KTU Quick Revision)

| Quantity | Final Expression | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Average Power | $P_{\text{avg}} = \text{DC} \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-\text{DC}) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$ | Lifetime estimation |
| TX Energy | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}}k + E_{\text{amp}}k\,d^{\alpha}$ | Energy-aware routing |
| RX Energy | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{\text{elec}}k$ | Energy-aware routing |
| Node Lifetime (days) | $L = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}} \cdot 86400}$ | Capacity planning |
| ADC Quantization Step | $Q = V_{\text{ref}}/2^{n}$ | Sensing accuracy |
| Duty Cycle | $\text{DC} = T_{\text{active}} / (T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}})$ | MAC design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Node — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates the **data path** (solid arrows) and the **power distribution path** (dashed arrows) inside a single sensor node.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nSENS["Sensing Unit - Sensor Transducer"] --> nADC["ADC"]
    nADC --> nCPU["Processing Unit - Microcontroller"]
    nCPU <--> nMEM["Memory - Flash RAM"]
    nCPU --> nRADIO["Transceiver Unit - RF Radio"]
    nRADIO <--> nANT["Antenna - Wireless Medium"]
    nBAT["Power Supply Unit - Battery"] -.-> nSENS
    nBAT -.-> nADC
    nBAT -.-> nCPU
    nBAT -.-> nMEM
    nBAT -.-> nRADIO
    nBAT -.-> nANT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal **top row** traces the *sensing → processing → communication* data pipeline.
- The **bottom row** shows how a single battery rail powers every subunit.

## 4.2 State Machine — Transceiver Power States

A radio can be in only **one** of the four states shown below. Transitions are driven by the MAC protocol.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nSLEEP
    nSLEEP --> nIDLE : Timer Interrupt
    nIDLE --> nTX : CSMA Backoff OK
    nIDLE --> nRX : Address Match
    nTX --> nIDLE : TX Complete
    nRX --> nIDLE : Packet Received
    nIDLE --> nSLEEP : No Event for T_wait
    nRX --> nSLEEP : No Address Match
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Sleep → Idle* is the wake-up edge (costs ~ µs).
- *Idle → TX/RX* depends on MAC CSMA/CA arbitration.
- The *Idle → Sleep* transition is the **key energy-saving edge**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Energy View)

This block diagram represents the **partitioning of total node energy** into its three macroscopic contributors — the most common analytical frame used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nTOT["TOTAL NODE ENERGY E_total"] --> nS["E_sense - Sensing Subunit"]
    nTOT --> nP["E_process - Processing Subunit"]
    nTOT --> nC["E_comm - Communication Subunit"]
    nC --> nCT["E_TX - Transmit Sub-energy"]
    nC --> nCR["E_RX - Receive Sub-energy"]
    nCR --> nCIdle["E_Idle - Listening Energy"]
    nCT --> nCsleep["E_Startup - Tail Energy"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical % share (TelosB mote, 1 packet/min):**
> - $E_{\text{sense}} \approx 5\%$
> - $E_{\text{process}} \approx 10\%$
> - $E_{\text{comm}} \approx 85\%$ — communication is the **dominant cost**, justifying MAC and routing energy optimizations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the main hardware components of a sensor node.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):**

A sensor node consists of **four functional subunits**:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Includes the physical sensor (e.g., temperature, humidity) and an ADC to convert analog signals to digital. (1 mark)
2. **Processing Unit** — Comprises a microcontroller and on-chip memory (Flash for program, RAM for data) to execute application logic. (1 mark)
3. **Transceiver Unit** — A short-range RF radio responsible for wireless data exchange with neighbouring nodes. (½ mark)
4. **Power Unit** — A battery (or energy harvester) that supplies DC power to all subunits. (½ mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students often forget to mention the ADC as part of the sensing unit — this costs ½ mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is duty cycling in WSNs? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Duty cycling is the technique of periodically switching the node (and its radio) between **active** and **sleep** states, such that energy consumption is minimized during idle periods. (1½ marks)

**Importance:** Since radio idle-listening consumes nearly as much power as reception, keeping the radio always-on would deplete the battery in days. Duty cycling can extend node lifetime from **days to years** — without it, WSNs are economically unviable. (1½ marks)

Mathematically: $\text{DC} = T_{\text{active}} / (T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}})$. For TelosB motes, a DC of **0.5 %–2 %** is typical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (CRITICAL):**
> When answering energy calculations, students frequently:
> 1. **Mix up** $E_{\text{elec}}$ and $E_{\text{amp}}$ — keep electronics (J/bit) and amplifier (J/bit/m²) clearly labelled.
> 2. **Forget units** — examiners award 0 marks for bare numerics with no units.
> 3. **Skip the boundary check** — always verify DC=1 gives $P_{\text{avg}}=P_{\text{active}}$.
> 4. **Confuse** path-loss exponent $\alpha$ with absorption coefficient — they are entirely different quantities.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1, 14 marks]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **block diagram of a sensor node**, clearly showing the four subunits and their interconnections. List any **two** operating systems used in WSNs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sensor node consumes **60 mW** when active and **30 µW** when asleep. The node operates with a **1% duty cycle** and runs a single **3 V / 1500 mAh** AA alkaline battery. Calculate:
   (i) Average power consumption. (3 marks)
   (ii) Estimated node lifetime in days, assuming the battery delivers 3 V nominal. (4 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

| Subunit | Function | Mark |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing Unit | Physical sensor + ADC; converts environmental stimuli into digital samples. | 1½ |
| Processing Unit | Microcontroller + memory; runs OS and application logic. | 1½ |
| Transceiver Unit | RF radio (TX, RX, Idle, Sleep states) for wireless communication. | 1½ |
| Power Unit | Battery or energy harvester; supplies regulated DC voltage to all subunits. | 1½ |
| Two OS examples | **TinyOS** (component-based, nesC) and **Contiki** (protothreads, IPv6). | 1 |

A clean block diagram similar to **Section 4.1** earns the full marks.

### Part (b) — Energy Calculations (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $P_{\text{active}} = 60 \text{ mW} = 60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$
- $P_{\text{sleep}} = 30 \text{ µW} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$
- $\text{DC} = 1\% = 0.01$
- Battery: $V = 3 \text{ V}$, capacity $C = 1500 \text{ mAh}$

#### (i) Average Power Consumption (3 marks)

Using the derived formula:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \text{DC} \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-\text{DC}) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} &= (0.01)(60 \times 10^{-3}) + (0.99)(30 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 0.6 \times 10^{-3} + 29.7 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 600 \text{ µW} + 29.7 \text{ µW} \\[4pt]
&= 629.7 \text{ µW} \\[4pt]
&\approx 630 \text{ µW} = 0.63 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** The sleep power contributes only ~4.7 % of average power — confirming that the duty cycle is the dominant lever.

#### (ii) Node Lifetime in Days (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Battery energy in Joules:**

$$E_{\text{bat}} = V \cdot Q = 3 \text{ V} \times 1500 \text{ mAh}$$

Convert to Coulombs: $1500 \text{ mAh} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 5.4 \text{ C}$ (per second-of-discharge equivalent).

Better: $E_{\text{bat}} = V \cdot C \cdot 3600$ where $C$ is in A·h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{bat}} &= 3 \text{ V} \times (1500 \times 10^{-3}) \text{ A} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 3 \times 1.5 \times 3600 \text{ W·s} \\[4pt]
&= 16200 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifetime in seconds:**

$$t = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{16200}{630 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ seconds}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{16200}{6.3 \times 10^{-4}} \\[4pt]
&= 2.571 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to days:**

$$L = \frac{2.571 \times 10^{7}}{86400} \approx 297.6 \text{ days} \approx 298 \text{ days}$$

> **Conclusion:** With 1 % duty cycle, the node lasts approximately **10 months** on a single AA cell — a practically viable deployment lifetime.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 1, 14 marks]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What is a **Wireless Sensor Network**? Compare its characteristics with traditional **ad-hoc networks** in any **four** dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Heinzelman energy model** for transmit and receive energy. A node transmits a **4000-bit** packet to a neighbour **8 m** away. Given $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{\text{amp}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $\alpha = 2$, calculate the **total energy consumed** for transmission. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — WSN vs. Ad-hoc Networks (7 marks)

A **Wireless Sensor Network** is a self-configuring network of densely deployed, energy-constrained sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions.

| Dimension | WSN | Traditional Ad-hoc (e.g., MANET) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy** | Battery-powered, energy is paramount | Rechargeable or mains-powered |
| **Node Count** | Hundreds to thousands | Tens, rarely more |
| **Mobility** | Mostly static after deployment | Highly mobile |
| **Communication Pattern** | Mostly sensor→sink (many-to-one) | Peer-to-peer, any-to-any |
| **Data Type** | Periodic or event-driven small packets | Multimedia, large bursts |
| **Role** | Sensing + forwarding | Forwarding only |

(Marks: 1 each for any four correct comparisons, plus 3 marks for the WSN definition with examples.)

### Part (b) — Heinzelman Model (7 marks)

**Standard Energy Model (Heinzelman et al., 2000):**

$$
E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + E_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}
$$

$$
E_{RX}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k
$$

**Given:**
- $k = 4000$ bits
- $d = 8$ m, $\alpha = 2$
- $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $E_{\text{amp}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² $= 100 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²

**Substitute into $E_{TX}$ formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TX} &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (100 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(8^{2}) \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times 10^{-6} + (100 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(64) \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times 10^{-6} + 25.6 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 225.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} \\[4pt]
&= 225.6 \text{ µJ} \approx 226 \text{ µJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The energy required is **225.6 µJ**. Note that even at 8 m, communication energy **dominates** electronics energy by **12.8 %** — and at longer distances this disparity grows as $d^{2}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A sensor node has **4 subunits**: Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, Power.
- ✅ The **transceiver** dominates energy consumption (~85 % of total).
- ✅ **Duty cycling** is the master technique to extend node lifetime — keeps radio off most of the time.
- ✅ **Heinzelman model:** $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}}k + E_{\text{amp}}k\,d^{\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Receive energy** = $E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k$ only — no amplifier term.
- ✅ **Average power:** $P_{\text{avg}} = \text{DC} \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-\text{DC}) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$.
- ✅ **Sleep power** is typically **3 orders of magnitude smaller** than active power.
- ✅ **TinyOS** and **Contiki** are the most cited WSN operating systems in KTU exams.
- ✅ **ADC quantization step** $Q = V_{\text{ref}} / 2^{n}$ — high $n$ ⇒ finer sensing.
- ✅ **Typical motes:** MicaZ, TelosB, IRIS, Sun SPOT.
- ✅ **Path-loss exponent $\alpha$** values: 2 (free space), 3–4 (urban), 4–6 (indoor obstructed).
- ✅ **Lifetime formula:** $L = E_{\text{bat}} / (P_{\text{avg}} \cdot 86400)$ days.
- ✅ Always **state units** in exam answers; bare numbers without units get partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hardware Components # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardware Components of a Wireless Sensor Node:** > A *sensor node* (also called a *mote*) is a miniaturised, resource-constrained embedded device that integrates four fundamental hardware subsystems — **(i) Sensing Unit**, **(ii) Processing Unit**, **(iii) Communication (Transceiver) Unit**, and **(iv) Power Unit** — onto a single low-power board to perform unattended sensing, in-situ computation, and short-range wireless communication in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). The hardware architecture of a sensor node is the physical realisation of the *Sense → Compute → Communicate* paradigm. Each subsystem is tightly coupled through an internal bus (typically SPI or I²C) and is governed by strict energy, size, and cost constraints dictated by the deployment environment (volcanic monitoring, structural health monitoring, precision agriculture, body-area networks, etc.). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Sensor Node is a "Miniature Field Biologist"** > > Imagine a tiny autonomous scientist dropped into a forest to monitor micro-climate. > > - **Sensing Unit** → The scientist's *eyes, ears, and skin* (sensors capture physical phenomena; ADC translates them into numbers). > - **Processing Unit** → The scientist's *brain* (microcontroller decides "is this reading worth transmitting?"). > - **Communication Unit** → The scientist's *walkie-talkie* (radio transceiver sends/reports critical data to base station). > - **Power Unit** → The scientist's *stomach / food supply* (battery + energy harvester; lifetime of node = lifetime of food). > > Just like the scientist cannot carry heavy gear or eat forever, a sensor node is severely limited in **memory (KB–MB)**, **processing (MHz)**, **bandwidth (kbps–Mbps)**, and **energy (Joules)**. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants The following are the standard hardware benchmarks every KTU examiner expects you to remember: - **Typical mote power budget:** $P_{node} \approx 30\text{ mW}$ (active) and $\approx 30\text{ μW}$ (sleep). - **Typical radio range:** $\mathbf{10\text{ m} - 100\text{ m}}$ for indoor WSN deployments. - **Operating frequency bands:** **2.4 GHz ISM band** (IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee) — licence-free worldwide. - **Standard battery capacity:** **2 × AA Alkaline** ($\approx 2 \times 2850\text{ mAh} = 5700\text{ mAh}$ at $3\text{ V}$). - **ADC resolution on commercial motes:** **10-bit** (e.g., Mica2) and **12-bit** (e.g., TelosB / Tmote Sky). ## 1.4 The Four Subsystems — At a Glance | Subsystem | Core Hardware | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | Sensing Unit | Physical sensor + ADC | Convert physical signal → digital data | | Processing Unit | Microcontroller + Memory | Local computation, scheduling | | Transceiver Unit | Radio (RF front-end) + antenna | Wireless data exchange | | Power Unit | Battery + DC-DC regulator + harvester | Energy provisioning & management | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Subsystem by Subsystem ### A. Sensing Unit (The "Senses") The sensing unit comprises **two** sub-blocks: 1. **Sensor (Transducer)** — converts physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, humidity, light, vibration, gas concentration) into a measurable analog electrical quantity, usually a *voltage* or *current*. 2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — discretises the analog signal into a binary representation usable by the microcontroller. **Classification of Sensors (must-know for KTU):** - **Active vs. Passive:** Active sensors require external excitation (e.g., radar); passive sensors self-generate a signal (e.g., thermocouple, photodiode). - **Scalar vs. Vector:** Scalar sensors measure magnitude only (temperature); vector sensors measure direction too (magnetometer, accelerometer). - **Analog vs. Digital:** Analog sensors output continuous voltage (LM35); digital sensors output bits (DHT11, SHT21). - **On-board vs. Off-board:** Modern motes (TelosB) support both stacked daughter boards. ### B. Processing Unit (The "Brain") Two architectural families dominate WSN hardware: - **Microcontroller-based (MCU):** e.g., ATmega128L on Mica2, MSP430F1611 on TelosB. **Low power (a few mW)**, **8/16-bit**, KB-level RAM. → *Energy-efficient for long-life deployments.* - **System-on-Chip / Stronger CPU:** e.g., ARM7 on Imote2, XScale PXA271. **32-bit**, MB-level RAM. → *Used for vision / multimedia WSN.* **Memory hierarchy:** - **Program Flash:** Mica2 = 128 KB, TelosB = 48 KB. - **RAM (data):** Mica2 = 4 KB, TelosB = 10 KB. - **External Flash (bulk storage):** Mica2 = 512 KB. ### C. Transceiver (Communication) Unit The radio is the **single largest power consumer** in a typical mote. Key parameters to remember: - **Operating band:** Mica2 = 433/868 MHz (CC1000); MicaZ/TelosB = 2.4 GHz (CC2420 — IEEE 802.15.4 compliant). - **Duty cycling:** Radio is OFF (sleep) $\approx 99\%$ of the time to conserve energy. - **Four operational states:** *Transmit*, *Receive*, *Idle*, *Sleep* — with widely different current draws (e.g., CC2420: TX $\approx 17.4\text{ mA}$, RX $\approx 19.7\text{ mA}$, Sleep $\approx 1\text{ μA}$). ### D. Power Unit Three sub-elements: 1. **Battery:** Alkaline, Lithium (Li-Ion / Li-Poly). Energy density is the critical metric. 2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Regulator:** Steps battery voltage (e.g., 3 V) to logic levels (e.g., 1.8 V, 3.3 V). Switching converters are preferred for efficiency $\eta > 85\%$. 3. **Energy Harvester (optional):** Solar cell, vibration piezo, thermoelectric, RF harvesting. ## 2.2 Why and How Each Subsystem Matters - **Why Sensing first?** Because without physical capture, no data exists. - **Why ADC in the sensing unit and not in the MCU?** Early ADCs were off-chip; modern MCUs integrate ADC. But for high-precision sensors (e.g., seismic), external 16/24-bit ADCs are still used. - **Why a dedicated radio?** Because WSNs are *wireless* by definition. - **Why is the power unit the most critical?** Because battery replacement in field deployments is **infeasible** — node lifetime essentially equals battery life. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarises **every key formula** examiners expect for Module 1 — Hardware Components. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` to keep markdown tables intact (no raw `|` symbols). | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ADC Quantisation Step (Resolution) | $V_q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_q$: 1 LSB voltage; $V_{FS}$: full-scale input range; $n$: ADC bit-width | Volts | | 2 | Quantisation Noise (RMS) | $V_{n,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_q}{\sqrt{12}}$ | RMS quantisation error of an ideal ADC | Volts | | 3 | Signal-to-Quantisation-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR for a full-scale sinusoid | dB | | 4 | Node Energy Consumption | $E_{node} = P_{avg} \cdot t$ | $P_{avg}$: average power drawn; $t$: operation time | Joules | | 5 | Battery Lifetime | $t_{life} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}}$ | $E_{batt}$: battery stored energy; $P_{avg}$: avg. system power | seconds | | 6 | Battery Capacity Conversion | $E_{batt} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600$ | Convert Ah → Joules via rated voltage | Joules | | 7 | Average Current (Duty-Cycled) | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{t_{on}\,I_{on} + t_{off}\,I_{off}}{T}$ | Duty-cycled radio/sensor current | Amperes | | 8 | DC-DC Converter Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Power transfer efficiency | % | | 9 | Shannon Channel Capacity (radio link) | $C = B\,\log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$: bandwidth; $S/N$: signal-to-noise ratio | bits/s | | 10 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $L_{fs}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(d_{km}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(f_{MHz}\right)$ | $d_{km}$: distance (km); $f_{MHz}$: frequency (MHz) | dB | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The hardware taxonomy above is what governs the design of every commercial IoT device you interact with: - **Smart agriculture** (e.g., Libelium Waspmote): multi-sensor board with solar harvester. - **Structural health monitoring** (e.g., Imote2 + MEMS accelerometer on Golden Gate Bridge). - **Body-area networks** (e.g., TelosB-based ECG patch for cardiac monitoring). - **Industrial IoT** (e.g., TI CC2650 SensorTag): integrates 10 sensors in a coin-cell device. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantisation Voltage of an Ideal ADC ### Problem Context A temperature sensor outputs $0\text{ V}$ to $5\text{ V}$ corresponding to $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$. The mote uses a 10-bit ADC. Find (a) the voltage resolution per LSB, and (b) the temperature resolution. ### Step-by-Step Working **Step 1 — Identify parameters.** - Full-scale range: $V_{FS} = 5\text{ V}$. - Bit-width: $n = 10$. - Number of discrete levels: $2^n = 2^{10} = 1024$. **Step 2 — Apply the resolution formula.** $$ V_q \;=\; \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}-1} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$ V_q \;=\; \dfrac{5\text{ V}}{1024 - 1} \;=\; \dfrac{5}{1023}\text{ V} $$ $$ V_q \;\approx\; 4.888 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V} \;=\; 4.89\text{ mV / LSB} $$ **Step 4 — Convert voltage resolution to temperature resolution.** - Sensor span: $125 - (-40) = 165^{\circ}\text{C}$. - Sensitivity: $\dfrac{5\text{ V}}{165^{\circ}\text{C}} \approx 30.3\text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$. $$ T_{res} \;=\; \dfrac{V_q}{\text{Sensitivity}} \;=\; \dfrac{4.89\text{ mV}}{30.3\text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}} $$ $$ T_{res} \;\approx\; 0.161^{\circ}\text{C / LSB} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** Always use $2^n - 1$ in the denominator (not $2^n$). The denominator represents *intervals*, not levels. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical SQNR of an ADC ### Step-by-Step Working **Step 1 — RMS signal of a full-scale sinusoid.** $$ V_{sig,\text{rms}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_q \cdot 2^{n-1}}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Step 2 — RMS quantisation noise.** $$ V_{n,\text{rms}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_q}{\sqrt{12}} $$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $\text{SQNR}$.** $$ \text{SQNR} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{sig,\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{V_{n,\text{rms}}^{\,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{V_q \cdot 2^{n-1}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{V_q}{\sqrt{12}}\right)^2} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $V_q^{\,2}$.** $$ \text{SQNR} \;=\; \dfrac{2^{2n-2} \cdot 12}{2} \;=\; 2^{2n} \cdot \dfrac{12}{4} \;=\; 2^{2n} \cdot 3 $$ **Step 5 — Take $10\log_{10}$.** $$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(3) + 20n\log_{10}(2) $$ Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$ and $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$: $$ \boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \;\approx\; 6.02\,n + 1.76\;} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Lifetime of a Duty-Cycled Mote ### Problem Context A Mica2 mote draws $I_{on} = 20\text{ mA}$ while transmitting (radio + MCU active) for $t_{on} = 5\text{ ms}$ each period, and $I_{sleep} = 30\text{ μA}$ otherwise. Period $T = 1\text{ s}$. Battery: $2 \times$ AA alkaline, $V_{bat} = 3\text{ V}$, $C_{Ah} = 2850\text{ mAh}$. Estimate lifetime in days. ### Step-by-Step Working **Step 1 — Average current (weighted by duty cycle).** $$ I_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{on}\,I_{on} + (T - t_{on})\,I_{sleep}}{T} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ I_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(20 \times 10^{-3}) + (0.995)(30 \times 10^{-6})}{1} $$ $$ I_{avg} \;=\; (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) + (2.985 \times 10^{-5}) $$ $$ I_{avg} \;\approx\; 1.2985 \times 10^{-4}\text{ A} \;\approx\; 0.1299\text{ mA} $$ **Step 3 — Convert battery capacity to Coulombs / Joules.** - Charge available: $Q = 2850\text{ mAh} = 2.85\text{ Ah}$. - Energy: $E_{batt} = V_{bat} \cdot Q \cdot 3600$. $$ E_{batt} \;=\; 3\text{ V} \times 2.85\text{ Ah} \times 3600\text{ s/h} $$ $$ E_{batt} \;=\; 3 \times 2.85 \times 3600 \;\approx\; 30{,}780\text{ J} $$ **Step 4 — Average power drawn.** $$ P_{avg} \;=\; V_{bat} \cdot I_{avg} \;=\; 3 \times 0.1299 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ P_{avg} \;\approx\; 3.896 \times 10^{-4}\text{ W} $$ **Step 5 — Lifetime in seconds, then days.** $$ t_{life} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{30{,}780}{3.896 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ t_{life} \;\approx\; 7.90 \times 10^{7}\text{ s} $$ Converting to days: $$ t_{life} \;\approx\; \dfrac{7.90 \times 10^{7}}{86{,}400} \;\approx\; 914\text{ days} \;\approx\; 2.5\text{ years} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** This is why **duty cycling** is the single most important energy-saving technique in WSNs. Notice how the **sleep current** (30 μA) is orders of magnitude smaller than active current (20 mA), so the mote spends > 99.5 % of its time asleep. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation) ### Step-by-Step Working **Step 1 — Friis equation in linear form.** $$ \dfrac{P_r}{P_t} \;=\; G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to dB.** $$ P_r(\text{dBm}) \;=\; P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) + G_r(\text{dBi}) - L_{fs}(\text{dB}) $$ **Step 3 — Express $L_{fs}$ in dB using frequency $f$ (Hz) and distance $d$ (m).** $$ L_{fs}(\text{dB}) \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) $$ Using $\lambda = c / f$ and converting $d$ to km and $f$ to MHz: $$ L_{fs}(\text{dB}) \;=\; 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) $$ **Step 4 — Numerical example.** - $f = 2.4\text{ GHz} = 2400\text{ MHz}$, $d = 50\text{ m} = 0.05\text{ km}$. $$ L_{fs} \;=\; 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(2400) $$ $$ L_{fs} \;=\; 32.44 + (-26.02) + 67.60 \;\approx\; 74.0\text{ dB} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sensor Node Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR PS[Power Unit
Battery + DC-DC + Harvester]:::power SU[Sensing Unit
Physical Sensor + ADC]:::sense PU[Processing Unit
Microcontroller + Memory]:::proc TU[Transceiver Unit
Radio RF + Antenna]:::tx EXT[External World
Base Station / Neighbour Node]:::ext PS -. supplies .-> SU PS -. supplies .-> PU PS -. supplies .-> TU SU -- raw samples --> PU PU -- processed packets --> TU TU -- wireless frames --> EXT EXT -- acknowledgements --> TU TU -- incoming data --> PU PU -- control commands --> SU classDef power fill:#FFE9A8,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sense fill:#CFE9FF,stroke:#0B4A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#1F6F1F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tx fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#8A0B0B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ext fill:#E6E6E6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Subsystem Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSING[ Sensing Unit ] A1[Physical Sensor
Temperature / Humidity / Vibration] --> A2[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier + Filter] A2 --> A3[Analog to Digital Converter
10 to 16 bit ADC] end subgraph PROCESSING[ Processing Unit ] B1[Microcontroller
MCU 8 or 16 or 32 bit] --> B2[Program Flash ROM] B1 --> B3[RAM Working Memory] B1 --> B4[External Flash Storage] end subgraph TRANSCEIVER[ Communication Unit ] C1[Baseband Modem
OQPSK or BPSK] --> C2[RF Front End
Transmit + Receive] C2 --> C3[Antenna
Whip or PCB Trace] end subgraph POWER[ Power Unit ] D1[Battery Cell
Alkaline or Li-Ion] --> D2[DC DC Voltage Regulator] D2 --> D3[Power Management IC
Duty Cycle Controller] D4[Energy Harvester
Solar or Piezo or Thermal] --> D2 end A3 -- digital samples --> B1 B1 -- processed packet --> C1 C3 -. over the air .-> EXT2[Neighbour Node / Sink] D3 -. enables .-> A1 D3 -. enables .-> B1 D3 -. enables .-> C1 classDef senseFill fill:#CFE9FF,stroke:#0B4A8A,color:#000 classDef procFill fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#1F6F1F,color:#000 classDef txFill fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#8A0B0B,color:#000 classDef pwFill fill:#FFE9A8,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 class A1,A2,A3 senseFill class B1,B2,B3,B4 procFill class C1,C2,C3 txFill class D1,D2,D3,D4 pwFill ``` ## 4.3 Commercial Mote Hardware Comparison Table | Mote Platform | MCU | Radio | Frequency | ADC | Flash / RAM | Power Source | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mica2 | ATmega128L (8-bit) | CC1000 | 433 / 868 MHz | 10-bit | 128 KB / 4 KB | 2 × AA | | MicaZ | ATmega128L | CC2420 (IEEE 802.15.4) | 2.4 GHz | 10-bit | 128 KB / 4 KB | 2 × AA | | TelosB / Tmote Sky | MSP430F1611 (16-bit) | CC2420 | 2.4 GHz | 12-bit | 48 KB / 10 KB | 2 × AA or USB | | BTnode | ATmega128L + Bluetooth | CC1000 + BT | 0.9 GHz + 2.4 GHz | 10-bit | 128 KB / 4 KB | External Li-Ion | | Imote2 | Intel PXA271 (32-bit ARM/XScale) | CC2420 | 2.4 GHz | — | 32 MB / 256 KB + 32 MB SDRAM | 3 × AAA | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Answer each question in approximately 60–80 words. KTU expects crisp definitions with one numerical example where applicable.** ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — 3 Marks **List the four fundamental hardware subsystems of a wireless sensor node. State one example hardware component for each.** **Model Answer:** A wireless sensor node consists of four subsystems: 1. **Sensing Unit** — example: LM35 temperature sensor + 10-bit ADC. 2. **Processing Unit** — example: ATmega128L microcontroller with 128 KB flash. 3. **Communication (Transceiver) Unit** — example: CC2420 radio operating at 2.4 GHz (IEEE 802.15.4). 4. **Power Unit** — example: $2 \times$ AA alkaline cells delivering $\approx 3\text{ V}$ at $2850\text{ mAh}$. These four subsystems together enable sense-compute-communicate operations in WSNs. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — 3 Marks **Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor | |---|---|---| | External excitation | Required | Not required | | Power consumption | Higher | Lower | | Example | Radar / ultrasonic sensor | Thermocouple / photodiode | Active sensors emit energy and measure the reflected response; passive sensors directly transduce ambient energy into an electrical signal. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 1 internal choice means you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the hardware architecture of a wireless sensor node. Explain the function of each subsystem. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor with full-scale range $0\text{ V}$ to $4.096\text{ V}$ is interfaced to a 12-bit ADC in a TelosB mote. Calculate (i) the voltage resolution per LSB, and (ii) the SQNR in dB. Comment on why higher ADC resolution is preferred for precision sensing. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The architecture of a sensor node comprises four tightly-coupled subsystems: 1. **Sensing Unit** — Contains the physical sensor (e.g., SHT21 for humidity) and an ADC. The sensor produces an analog voltage proportional to the physical quantity; the ADC digitises it. 2. **Processing Unit** — Built around a microcontroller (e.g., MSP430F1611 on TelosB). It runs the OS (TinyOS), executes sensing schedule, performs local aggregation, and stores data in flash/RAM. 3. **Transceiver Unit** — A short-range radio (e.g., CC2420 at 2.4 GHz) handles packet transmission and reception compliant with IEEE 802.15.4. 4. **Power Unit** — Battery + DC-DC regulator + optional harvester. Powers the other three subsystems and enforces duty cycling. These four units are interconnected by an internal bus, and the entire design is optimised for **energy efficiency** because battery replacement is infeasible. *(Include the block diagram from Section 4.1 in your answer sheet.)* #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Voltage resolution per LSB.** $$ V_q \;=\; \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}-1} \;=\; \dfrac{4.096}{2^{12}-1} \;=\; \dfrac{4.096}{4095} $$ $$ V_q \;\approx\; 1.00\text{ mV / LSB} $$ **(ii) Signal-to-Quantisation-Noise Ratio.** $$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 6.02\,n + 1.76 \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 $$ $$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \;\approx\; 74.0\text{ dB} $$ **Comment:** Higher resolution (larger $n$) gives a finer $V_q$ (better discrimination between close physical values) and a higher SQNR (less quantisation noise). For precision sensing — seismic, structural health, biomedical — 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs are preferred over the mote's 12-bit internal ADC. --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Compare the hardware specifications of Mica2, MicaZ, and TelosB motes. Which of these is most energy-efficient and why? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Mica2 mote operates in duty-cycled mode. The active current is $I_{on} = 25\text{ mA}$ (radio + MCU), and the sleep current is $I_{sleep} = 20\text{ μA}$. The radio is ON for $t_{on} = 10\text{ ms}$ every $T = 2\text{ s}$ cycle. Battery: $V_{bat} = 3\text{ V}$, $C = 2500\text{ mAh}$. Estimate the lifetime in years. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): | Spec | Mica2 | MicaZ | TelosB | |---|---|---|---| | MCU | ATmega128L (8-bit) | ATmega128L (8-bit) | MSP430F1611 (16-bit) | | Radio | CC1000 | CC2420 (802.15.4) | CC2420 (802.15.4) | | Frequency | 433/868 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | | Flash / RAM | 128 KB / 4 KB | 128 KB / 4 KB | 48 KB / 10 KB | | ADC | 10-bit | 10-bit | 12-bit | | Active current | $\sim 20\text{ mA}$ | $\sim 25\text{ mA}$ | $\sim 22\text{ mA}$ | | Sleep current | $\sim 30\text{ μA}$ | $\sim 30\text{ μA}$ | $\sim 6\text{ μA}$ | **TelosB is the most energy-efficient** because of its ultra-low-power MSP430 MCU (16-bit RISC architecture with five low-power modes), deeper integration (single-chip radio + MCU on shared SPI bus), and the lowest sleep current (6 μA vs 30 μA on Mica). This allows TelosB to achieve > 1 year lifetime on a pair of AA cells. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Average current.** $$ I_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{on}\,I_{on} + (T - t_{on})\,I_{sleep}}{T} $$ $$ I_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.010)(25\times 10^{-3}) + (1.990)(20\times 10^{-6})}{2} $$ $$ I_{avg} \;=\; (1.25\times 10^{-4}) + (1.99\times 10^{-5}) \;\approx\; 1.449 \times 10^{-4}\text{ A} \;\approx\; 0.1449\text{ mA} $$ **Step 2 — Battery energy.** $$ E_{batt} \;=\; V_{bat}\cdot C\cdot 3600 \;=\; 3 \times 2.5 \times 3600 \;=\; 27{,}000\text{ J} $$ **Step 3 — Average power.** $$ P_{avg} \;=\; V_{bat}\cdot I_{avg} \;=\; 3 \times 0.1449 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 4.347\times 10^{-4}\text{ W} $$ **Step 4 — Lifetime.** $$ t_{life} \;=\; \dfrac{27{,}000}{4.347\times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 6.21\times 10^{7}\text{ s} $$ $$ t_{life} \;\approx\; \dfrac{6.21\times 10^{7}}{3.1536\times 10^{7}} \;\approx\; 1.97\text{ years} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Common mistake:** Students often use $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ in the ADC resolution formula — losing **1 mark**. Always use $2^n - 1$. > 2. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mAh → Joules before dividing by power in Watts — lose **2 marks** if you compute lifetime in seconds but report in years without proper conversion. > 3. **Duty cycle error:** Students frequently average only the ON current and ignore the sleep current — even though sleep current is much smaller, it is applied for $\approx 99.5\%$ of the period, so its contribution is **not negligible**. > 4. **Diagram marks:** In Part (a), the block diagram itself carries **2 marks** in KTU valuation. Skipping arrows or labelling is a guaranteed deduction. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hardware Components** - ☐ A WSN node has **exactly four subsystems**: Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power. - ☐ The **ADC** lives inside (or with) the Sensing Unit; its resolution is $V_q = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$. - ☐ **Theoretical SQNR** $= 6.02n + 1.76\text{ dB}$ — derived from quantisation noise. - ☐ The **radio (transceiver)** is the biggest energy consumer → therefore duty cycling is mandatory. - ☐ **Mica2** uses CC1000 (sub-GHz); **MicaZ / TelosB** use CC2420 (2.4 GHz, IEEE 802.15.4). - ☐ **TelosB** is the most energy-efficient commercial mote thanks to MSP430's low sleep current. - ☐ **Battery lifetime** $t_{life} = E_{batt} / P_{avg}$; always convert mAh → Joules using $E = V \cdot Q \cdot 3600$. - ☐ **Active vs. passive sensors:** active needs excitation; passive does not. - ☐ **Scalar vs. vector sensors:** scalar = magnitude only; vector = magnitude + direction. - ☐ **Shannon capacity** $C = B\,\log_2(1 + S/N)$ bounds the radio link throughput. - ☐ **Friis path loss** in dB: $L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$. - ☐ Memory sizes on classic motes are in **KB**, not MB — Imote2 is the exception with MB-scale RAM. - ☐ **Energy harvesters** (solar, piezo, thermal) extend node lifetime beyond battery capacity. - ☐ Exam trick: when asked "which subsystem is most critical?", answer **Power Unit** — because node lifetime = battery lifetime. - ☐ Always state the **operating band** and **modulation** when describing a radio (e.g., CC2420 @ 2.4 GHz, O-QPSK, IEEE 802.15.4).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**, *Network Characteristics* refer to the qualitative and quantitative attributes that fundamentally define the behavior, performance, scalability, and operational limits of the network formed by spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes. These characteristics dictate how the network senses, communicates, processes, and delivers information from the physical environment to the sink (base station).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMRT634, Module 1)**, Network Characteristics encompass the design-time and run-time properties that differentiate a WSN from traditional wired or ad-hoc networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Network Characteristics" in Module 1 of PEMRT634 broadly covers *network topology, network capacity, node deployment, energy efficiency, scalability, fault tolerance, latency, coverage, connectivity, mobility, and self-organization.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Intelligent Ant Colony":**
> Imagine a colony of thousands of tiny ants deployed across a vast forest to detect forest fires. Each ant (sensor node) is:
> - **Tiny & battery-powered** (limited energy, just like a coin cell on a MICAz mote).
> - **Autonomous** (no central boss; each ant follows simple rules → self-organization).
> - **Mobile/Static** (some ants crawl → mobility; others stay at fixed points → static deployment).
> - **Capable of multi-hop communication** (ant passes message to the next ant toward the queen → sink).
> - **Prone to failure** (an ant may die → fault tolerance required).

The *characteristics* of this ant colony (its network) determine whether the colony succeeds in alerting the queen in time. Similarly, the network characteristics determine whether a WSN can reliably, efficiently, and timely deliver sensory data.

### 1.3 Core Defining Parameters (at a Glance)

The **fundamental network characteristics** of a WSN are:

1. **Network Topology** — Star, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid
2. **Network Capacity / Throughput** — Bits per second the network can sustain
3. **Node Deployment Strategy** — Deterministic vs. Random
4. **Energy Consumption** — Joules per bit transmitted
5. **Scalability** — Ability to add nodes without performance degradation
6. **Fault Tolerance / Reliability** — Tolerance to node/link failures
7. **Coverage Area** — Physical region monitored by the network
8. **Network Connectivity** — Logical/graph-theoretic connectivity of nodes
9. **Latency / Response Time** — Delay between event detection and sink delivery
10. **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Application-specific performance guarantee
11. **Security** — Resistance to attacks, data integrity, confidentiality
12. **Mobility Support** — Static, mobile, or hybrid node scenarios
13. **Self-Organization / Self-Healing** — Autonomous topology management
14. **Heterogeneity** — Homogeneous vs. heterogeneous node types
15. **Data-centric Operation** — Routing based on data content, not node ID

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this KTU mantra:** *"WSN = Energy-Constrained + Data-Centric + Self-Organized + Fault-Tolerant + Multi-Hop."*

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Electromagnetic wave propagation speed in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Typical ISM band:** **2.4 GHz** (used by IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee)
- **Transmission range (typical outdoor):** **30–100 m** for IEEE 802.15.4 radios
- **Energy of a typical coin cell:** **~1.0 Ah @ 3 V ≈ 10,800 J** of total energy budget
- **Node density in dense WSN:** **10–20 nodes/m³** in some deployment scenarios
- **Packet size (IEEE 802.15.4):** **127 bytes** maximum PHY payload
